summary: DHH argues that programmers irrationally trust cloud and SaaS infrastructure over their own machines, a phenomenon he links to imposter syndrome. He uses the example of moving CI back to developer machines, where critics invoked 'it works on my machine' as if cloud computers were fundamentally different. He contends that all computers are equally fallible, testing is about confidence rather than certainty, and running tests on multiple developer machines is actually a feature that uncovers hidden dependencies. The post concludes that the perceived superiority of cloud operators is an illusion created by opaqueness, not actual competence.
key_points: Programmers suffer from a form of imposter syndrome that makes them trust remote infrastructure over their own machines
Cloud computers are not magically more reliable — they're just other computers with the same potential for faults
Running CI on multiple developer machines is a feature that helps uncover undeclared dependencies and environmental issues
Testing provides degrees of confidence, not certainty — the pursuit of 100% certainty leads to programmer misery
The perceived competence of cloud operators comes from opaqueness, not actual superiority
money_quotes: There are no magic machines and no magic operators. Just the same kind of potentially faulty bits and brains.
The computer running tests remotely is indeed just that: Another computer. It isn't magical, and it's no less prone to be reliant on unaccounted for dependencies or environmental factors.
When it comes to testing, it's a feature not a bug to have the suite run on multiple machines.
The road to programmer misery is paved with delusional aspirations that you can ever be fully, truly certain that any sufficiently complicated system will ever work as intended in production.
All you have is degrees of confidence to trade-off against increasingly cumbersome protocols and procedures.
If only you knew what kind of frazzled mechanical turk it takes to run most cloud institutions or SaaS operations, you wouldn't be so quick to doubt your own abilities.
"It works on my computer" is just the midwit version of "it works on THAT computer". It's all just computers.
key_insight: The trust programmers place in cloud infrastructure over their own machines is an illusion born from imposter syndrome and the opaqueness of remote operations, not from any real difference in reliability.
